metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Workplace Mathematics 11 deepens the practical mathematical skills developed in Workplace Mathematics 10, with a focus on financial literacy, personal budgeting, 3D object visualization, probability and statistics, and data interpretation. Students apply mathematics to authentic workplace, consumer, and social justice contexts. This course supports students planning to enter the workforce, trades, or community college programs.
metadata.general_description: A practical mathematics course applying financial reasoning, budgeting, 3D visualization, probability, statistics, and data literacy to workplace and everyday contexts — supporting applied, trades, and direct-to-workforce pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Proportional reasoning is used to make sense of multiplicative relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reasoning about relative size and scale allows us to make sense of rates, ratios, and comparisons in practical workplace and financial contexts.
Understanding interest, loans, insurance, and budgeting empowers individuals to make informed and sustainable financial decisions throughout their lives.

big_ideas.statement: 3D objects are often represented and described in 2D space.
big_ideas.elaboration: Understanding how three-dimensional objects are drawn and communicated in two dimensions — through perspective diagrams, exploded views, and scale drawings — is essential for trades and technical work.

big_ideas.statement: Flexibility with number builds meaning, understanding, and confidence.
big_ideas.elaboration: Fluency with numbers, estimation, and numerical reasoning builds the confidence to apply mathematics in unfamiliar real-world situations.

big_ideas.statement: Representing and analyzing data allows us to notice and wonder about relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reading, creating, and critically evaluating data representations allows us to draw conclusions about social, economic, and personal situations.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and play games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible, strategic thinking about number; model with mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively and with curiosity when exploring problems.
i_can_statements: I can develop and apply thinking strategies to solve practical and workplace mathematical problems.
I can model real-world financial and consumer situations with mathematics.
I can estimate reasonably in financial, statistical, and measurement contexts.
I can approach problems with curiosity and creative thinking.

competencies.raw: Develop, demonstrate, and apply conceptual understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore and illustrate mathematical concepts; apply flexible and strategic approaches to solve problems; solve problems with persistence and positive disposition; engage in problem-solving connected with place, story, cultural practices, and perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can solve financial, statistical, and 3D visualization problems using appropriate strategies.
I can visualize and sketch 3D objects from 2D diagrams and vice versa.
I can persist through challenging problems and connect my solutions to real-world contexts.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary in classroom discussions; take risks when offering ideas in classroom discourse.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify my mathematical reasoning in practical contexts.
I can represent financial and statistical information using tables, graphs, diagrams, and symbols.
I can use correct mathematical vocabulary when discussing financial and statistical problems.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts with each other, other areas, and personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, knowledge, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my problem-solving approaches and adjust my strategies.
I can connect mathematics to financial planning, career choices, and community contexts.
I can incorporate First Peoples perspectives on mathematics and economics into my thinking.

areas.name: Financial Literacy
areas.raw: Personal investments, loans, budgeting; vehicle ownership; banking services; significant purchases.
topics: Savings and investments: simple and compound interest, GICs, savings accounts
Loans and debt: interest calculations, monthly payments, total cost of borrowing
Budgeting: personal budget creation — income, fixed, variable, and discretionary expenses
Vehicle ownership: purchase vs. lease, insurance, fuel, and maintenance costs
Significant purchases: credit, financing, and comparison shopping
Banking services: accounts, fees, e-transfers, and responsible banking

areas.name: Rate of Change and Angles
areas.raw: Slope of 3D objects; angles of elevation; interest rates as rates of change.
topics: Slope in 3D contexts: roof pitch, ramp slope, and grade
Angles of elevation and depression: practical applications in trades and construction
Interest rates as rates of change: visual and numerical representations

areas.name: Probability and Statistics
areas.raw: Games of chance; insurance payout likelihood; survey interpretation; statistical vocabulary.
topics: Experimental probability: running simulations and games of chance
Theoretical probability: single and combined events
Insurance mathematics: expected value and risk assessment
Statistical vocabulary: population, sample, bias, margin of error
Interpreting surveys: identifying bias and limitations in data collection

areas.name: Data and Graph Interpretation
areas.raw: Interpreting and critiquing graphs in media; data influence on social justice issues.
topics: Types of graphs: bar, line, circle, pictograph, histogram
Reading and critiquing graphs in news media and advertising
How data is used to support or challenge social justice arguments
Creating and communicating data displays effectively

areas.name: 3D Object Representation
areas.raw: Exploded diagrams; perspective diagrams; drawing and constructing 3D objects.
topics: Perspective drawings: one-point and two-point perspective
Exploded diagrams: assembly instructions and technical drawings
Isometric drawing: representing 3D objects on grid paper
Scale drawings and models: creating and interpreting technical plans
